title: The best labs to get and how to interpret them

chunks.title: Introduction to Blood Work
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces the concept of blood work as a crucial tool for understanding one's health, emphasizing the fasting insulin test and the importance of specific labs that are often overlooked by mainstream physicians.
chunks.transcript: and I think there is no better test than fasting insulin I simply do not understand why more Physicians do not order a fasting insulin onto the podcast my friends welcome to the third installment of my blood work in the last six to seven months in this podcast I wanted to go over which blood work you might consider ordering on your own or with your physician who is willing to do blood work with you for you and how to think about that blood work how to interpret that blood work I think that blood work is a fascinating lens into what is going on in your body and it's not a perfect microscope of how healthy you are but as I will talk about in this

chunks.title: Diet Overview
chunks.summary: The speaker outlines his current diet, which includes grass-fed beef, raw dairy, and a variety of fruits. He highlights the nutritional composition of his meals and daily caloric intake.
chunks.transcript: so this is like I said the third episode in which I've talked about my blood work this is blood work from March of 2023. I did previous episodes August 2022 and November 2022 and this is in an effort to continue to open the kimono as it were and show you all what my blood work looks like as a 45 year old male taking no drugs no hormones no supplements I use hardened soil desiccated organs but I don't think of those as supplements as much as they are just desiccated organs in a capsule so if you think as a supplement then fine it's a supplement I end up taking beef organs and whole package throughout the week when I Can't Get Enough fresh organs or if I want some of the organs I'm not not getting enough of but that's all to say that you all know my diet for the most part I've done many videos about what I eat in a day I have meat grass-fed beef maybe a pound to a pound and a half a day it varies from day to day I have lots of raw dairy I have raw goat's milk right now raw yogurt raw kefir and I have fruit in Costa Rica I'm getting lots of mangoes papaya watermelon oranges I make orange juice I do local raw honey which is glyphosate free I have a low microplastic sea salt and that's basically my diet most of what I drink is coconut water and if you look at the previous videos I've done on what I eat in a day and putting that through chronometer you can see where the nutrients are that I'm eating and you can see the balance of saturated fat monounsaturated fat polyunsaturated fat obviously the latter are quite low you can see the amount of calories I get is probably over 3000 a day sometimes 3 500

chunks.title: Fasting Insulin and Prolactin Tests
chunks.summary: Discussion on fasting insulin and prolactin levels, where the speaker shares his recent test results, their significance, and how these hormones impact health metrics like stress and metabolic health.
chunks.transcript: mainstream Physicians which is why it's fun to get to do what I do I want this to be educational I wanted to make you curious and hopefully in the future there will be some way to enable actual healthy debate with some of the people in the health space whose opinions might differ from mine on this blood work on health metrics and how we become healthy as humans and I'll refer to that throughout the podcast so with that intro let's get into my recent blood work from March and we'll go from there so as we go through this I will show on the screen share which is going to be on YouTube and Spotify now has a video platform my blood work I'm showing you the actual unadulterated blood work from LabCorp where I got it I did this blood work through Merrick Health they're a sponsor the podcast I think they're great and they've been helpful to me and just ordering whatever blood work I want to get on my own so that's what we'll do and at the end of the podcast I will sort of run through all of the blood work I've done so you can save that part re-listen to it write it down well maybe we'll put it in the show notes we'll transcribe that part so you can look and get a list of all the things that I do now to be fair this blood work panel is fairly comprehensive and you may not need something that is quite this comprehensive if you have different goals in mind but when I do blood work on myself I want to get as much as possible and I find it quite fascinating to really dig into all of this so let's start with two blood tests that I think are some of the most important that you can do that are very rarely done by mainstream Physicians this is the fasting insulin and the prolactin and as you can see here I am a 45 year old male I was born June 29 1977. that's me this is my blood work so my insulin My fasting insulin was 3.2 micro IU per ML on this blood work which was in mid-march 2023 and my prolactin was 10.5 now for context usually almost every single time that I've done fasting insulin it's right around three micro IU per ml or lower but I did have previous blood work on the 6th of March which is why I repeated this one where I had a 5.8 out of nowhere so I don't know why my fasting insulin was up on the 6th of March I don't really vary my diet that much but this is just to say that if your blood work looks a little funny just repeat it sometimes things are strange in what we're doing the body is complex and we can't always go based on one snapshot I'll talk about that with regard to lipids later in this podcast as well so this is a outlying value for me a 5.8 is it a lab error was I just more tired I was traveling for all of this blood work so it's influenced by that I'm not sleeping in the same bed that I'm used to in Costa Rica which is where I spend most of my time and I'm frankly not my best self when I'm traveling because I'm not in my space I'm exposed to lice at Night My circadian rhythm is off and moving across time zones so take that into consideration when we're looking at these Labs but regardless um one of these fasting insulin levels was not horrible 5.8 is not horrible but if somebody came to me with a fasting incident 5.9 it's a it's a little higher than I'd like to see it so I repeated it 3.2 which is more like what I'm used to seeing you'll see here my prolactin was 11.8 on the 6th of March again I think that on the 6th of March I just really hadn't slept in well the night before prolactin tends to be a little bit elevated when you haven't slept well when you are fatigued and even this 10.5 from this mid-march blood work so I got blood work on the 6th of March and probably on the 15th or something of March and this 10.5 for blackton is also high for me I've had prolactins in the sevens before which is probably more of my normal why do I care about prolactin prolactin is important when you're thinking about dopamine and when you're thinking about testosterone I had a friend recently who sent me his blood work and his prolactin was 34 and I thought you need to work that up you need to look and see if you have a little adenoma in your brain that's not a normal level of prolactin in the human body especially for a male or any woman who is not breastfeeding so prolactin is something that can interfere with sex drive with erectile function with weight loss with libido all of these things and so you want to know what your prolactin is and it should kind of track with metabolic Health the thing to know about prolactin also is that it is elevated after an orgasm so if you've had sex or an orgasm the night before you get your blood work it might be a little higher but in metabolically healthy males specifically there should be a quick return to Baseline of prolactin after orgasm I imagine it's the same for women we can look into that data on a future podcast as well so start your blood work with a fasting insulin and a prolactin make sure you get those two know what levels are normal for you you definitely want your prolact and I would say less than 15 which is the reference interval and I would say probably less than 10 and understand like I said that if you are very tired fatigued or had sex right before this test it might not be a totally accurate measure of things but if you're prolactin is in the 30s or in the 20s or in the High Teens something is off there and you need to dig into that and if your insulin is really much above four or five then you need to think about what is causing insulin resistance and a theme throughout this podcast on blood work and as I talk about blood work in general and health of humans in general will be that there is no greater metric to understand than your insulin sensitivity and I think there is no better test than fasting insulin I

chunks.title: Cortisol and DHEA Ratio
chunks.summary: The speaker explains the cortisol to DHEA ratio as a measure of stress and potential longevity health. He discusses his cortisol levels and their implications, linking them to overall well-being.
chunks.transcript: is from March the 6th blood work my morning cortisol was 15.8 again previously my morning cortisol was 10.0 this is probably some indication of poor sleep as I'm traveling but my dheas is 126 and previously 139 micrograms per deciliter so this ratio cortisol to DHA Cas is perhaps the single easiest but also most powerful ratio to assess how stressed you are and to potentially assess your longevity or the trajectory that you are headed toward in your longevity what do you want this ratio to be less than 0.3 you can see that even in my stressed state with poor sleep my ratio is 0.124 so I'm not in a bad spot and the ratio was even lower when I was perhaps a little better slept in November 10 over 139 both of these are the same units micrograms per deciliter so understand what your cortisol is get an AM cortisol these are both am fasting cortisols and if you are really interested in knowing where your cortisol level is get it throughout the day because understanding your cortisol will give you many insights into your overall health and could help you

chunks.title: Hormonal Health Insights
chunks.summary: The focus shifts to testosterone and other sex hormones, where the speaker shares insights from his blood work results, emphasizing the relationship between diet and hormone levels.
chunks.transcript: you can see here that my testosterone on this test was 819 nanograms per deciliter free testosterone is 1.5 that's generally where I am I had a blood work test a few months ago where it was much higher at three I'm not sure why it was quite that high and then my free testosterone is 123 which is right in the middle of the reference range so as a reminder I'm 45 years old I take no hormonal supplementation I've never taken never taken trt anyone tells you that as a 40 year old man or 40 year old or a 50 year old man or woman your androgens and androgens are important for women too that these are just going to decline with age you call because this is not true I've seen this over and over I've worked with people specifically I had a I had I had a previous client text me the other day and say that his 71.5 year old testosterone was 820 or something essentially the same as mine so he's 72 almost and he has the same testosterone as mine so if you believe that testosterone declines with age as a man you're sort of being fed this BS narrative and I don't know what his free testosterone was off the top of my head but we could look into that so my testosterone has consistently been seven to eight hundred with zero supplementation I don't do any weird herbs or things that are going to have funny side effects I eat meat I eat organs I eat fruit I eat honey and it's fine and I don't have insulin resistance as I showed you with my passing insulin and as I'll show you with my liver tests I don't have any indications of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and there's actually no good evidence that fructose leads to non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and as I'll show you there is very compelling evidence that significant amounts of simple sugars will increase testosterone sperm motility testicular volume in human males so that's to say again back to the equation that I was talking about on one side of the equation you have cortisol which is scarcity and stress on the other side you have dheas which is Vitality libido fertility and the way that you support fertility is by giving your body enough carbohydrates so I'll get to that in a moment but one thing that I want to talk about that's interesting with my testosterone is the sex hormone binding globulin if you guys are familiar with my history and in connection with iron you'll understand the story but if you're not I will tell the story quickly so the basic gist here is that my sex hormone binding globulin which is a protein in the human blood like albumin that carries our sex hormones has been elevated for a long time and when I was on keto my shpg was in the 120s at one time so low carb appears to raise shpg this is an argument that I've advanced in the past that is probably that ketogenic low carb diets are not great for sex hormones because when you have more shpg you're going to have less free hormones in your body and so I was wondering why is my shpg so high I experimented with things like Boron didn't really change anything and I'm not sure that's the right solution either I think I'm getting plenty of boron in my diet I stumbled upon this idea that perhaps my shpg was elevated because perhaps I had a little bit too much iron in my blood as I've talked about in the past I don't have hemochromatosis I don't have genes for hemochromatosis I've shown my genes on previous podcasts but I do seem to hold on to iron when I eat meat every day and to be fair this is a reason that some people may argue Paul isn't this an impetus to eat less red meat with all of this bioavailable heme iron and I don't really think it is because here's the short answer I think red meat especially grass-fed red meat is probably the most evolutionarily consistent food we could eat because it's fed the most ancestrally evolutionarily consistent diet cows are fed grass throughout their lives and that grass is what they're supposed to be eating I don't really want to eat lower iron containing chicken that is fed corn and soy and Grains if I eat the dark meat I'm going to get a similar amount of heme iron as I am with red meat if I eat darker pork I'm going to get that but both pork and chicken are fed corn and soy and I don't want to eat those Foods I would rather eat red meat and then understand where my iron is and manage it how do I manage it well this may sound a little intense but I've been doing phlebotomy so this means I essentially drain a little bit of blood every week and if you've heard my previous podcasts what have I started doing yeah I do my own blood draws so every week stick a needle in my vein usually right there and I drain out 50 to 60 cc's of blood which I then give to that plant behind me which is maybe why it looks so healthy and as you can see my ferritin I'll show you the ferritin levels have gone down significantly with that process and interestingly and this is really the first time that I'm able to share this on blood work my shpg has now

chunks.title: Iron Management Strategies
chunks.summary: Discussion on iron levels, the management of hemoglobin and ferritin through phlebotomy, and how these factors can influence overall health, particularly testosterone levels.

holding and very specific attention into creating an animal-based diet which I think could be very helpful for many of you as well I wanted to talk a little bit about this idea of testosterone and how you can hurt it and how you can improve it so I don't find much of this that surprising but there is good evidence that things like soy probably lower testosterone flaxseed with its lignins which are studies to corroborate those assertions so here's a pilot study of dietary fat restriction and flaxseed supplementation in men with prostate cancer before surgery and prostate cancer is often used as a model of reducing androgens but flaxseed does reduce androgens big surprise there I wouldn't have that in my diet the effects of macronutrients on reproductive hormones and overweight and obese men a pilot study what you see in this study is that polyunsaturated fatty acids actually lowered testosterone that's not a good thing those are things like the omega-6 linoleic acid and Seed oils which are corn canola safflower sunflower Etc so again as if all of the previous discussion I'd had on seed oils wasn't enough if you want a healthy amount of testosterone you probably don't want to have significant amounts of seed oils in your diet and these things hide you can watch my content on Instagram Tick Tock wherever these things are in a lot of foods that you wouldn't expect them to be in inversely correlated with serum total testosterone after controlling for age energy body mass index alcohol smoking status correlation was statistically significant for polyunsaturated fat so more polyunsaturated fat lower serum total testosterone in Japanese males aged 43 to 88 years you can see here on YouTube if you want the actual reference of that study here's an interesting one the effects of peppermint teas on plasma testosterone follicle stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone levels FSH follicle simulating hormone and LH luteinizing hormone are the pituitary signals to the testicles to produce testosterone and other androgens and this is in testicular tissue in rats you want to have any guesses as to what happens it says that the herbs have some quote-unquote toxic effects in these rat testicles they say

chunks.title: Lipid Profile Evaluation
chunks.summary: The speaker evaluates his lipid profile, explaining the significance of various lipid markers and advocating for a nuanced understanding of cholesterol in the context of overall metabolic health.

the fact that I'm eating choline sometimes tmao is high in vegetarians again this is one of the worst metrics I put it on there just to show you guys how much it is and I should point out that there's atherosclerosis in veins and I haven't heard a good answer for that the answer that I hear is that we don't get atherosclerosis and veins because there's less pressure in veins well of course there's less pressure in veins because they're in the venous circulation versus the arterial circulation but it's that pressure in the arteries that damages the endothelium and that's what causes the beginning of atherosclerosis when the healing of the endothelium is impaired as it is when we're insulin resistant there's really no evidence that a higher pressure in arteries pushes apob particles into the sub-endothelial space that process is an active process by which LDL must move across the endothelium and we know that macrophages do not take up native LDL that is if an LDL particle or any other apob containing particle gets into your sub-endothelial space and is not oxidized macrophages will not take that up and macrophages must engulf oxidized LDL particles to make foam cells which are the beginning of a fatty streak and atherosclerosis so I believe that LDL is part of the healing process in those arteries and perhaps LDL is moved into the sub-endothelial space in an active way but that I really remain unconvinced and I've talked about this at length on previous podcasts about that LDL and apob are directly atherogenic it is all about the context which you can understand by knowing how insulin sensitive you are that was why I started this podcast with the very first metric of a fasting insulin so um again I don't know that it's worth going through this NMR panel my small ldlp is high because a fraction of your LDL will be small and if you have more LDL then is expected or if it meets the reference range you will trigger a high small LDL most of the cholesterol metrics are indicators of insulin resistance so we're using a poor secondary metric to get a sense of insulin resistance in fact if you look at this this NMR profile even gave me a lipoprotein insulin resistance score and it scored me high when we absolutely know that my insulin resistance is not existent because my fasting insulin is low and my argument here is that these NMR profiles are they're really they're too sensitive they're not specific enough for what we are looking for so by too sensitive I mean they're giving all sorts of triggers and saying there's lots of cardiovascular risk when I don't believe there's any real good correlation or indication of cardiovascular risk with what it's showing me here I think there are much better indicators of cardiovascular risk specifically things like elevated triglycerides elevated fasting insulin and overall insulin resistance and metabolic health so if you look back at my lipid panels again my LDL was 130 160 207 it varies a lot day to day and I think that it is high quote unquote because I eat lots of saturated fat in animal Foods Dairy cheese meat organs and very little polyunsaturated fat we know that according to this homeaviscus model and other uh compelling theories that there's pretty good evidence that when you eat more saturated fat your LDL goes up and all Physicians worry about this because we're all sort of sold on this false fake Paradigm that APO B is inherently atherogenic I don't believe it is I think that LDL is not a problem unless you are insulin resistant and LDL is not even a problem in itself it just gets wrapped up in the process of arterial healing again this is contrary to the mainstream perspective and I hope to have some Direct clear debates with uh people who feel the opposite way contributes to nafldi so I have low inflammation I am very insulin sensitive my lipids are in my opinion just fine

chunks.title: Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD) Discussion
chunks.summary: The speaker presents research on NAFLD, arguing against the misconception that fructose intake leads to this condition and linking it instead to factors like cortisol levels and dietary fat.
chunks.transcript: disease this is a fascinating article showing that excessive gluconeogenesis causes hepatic insulin resistance Paradox and it's sequela the sequela which is a fancy word for the downstream effects are non-alcoholic fatty liver disease well what causes excessive gluconeogenesis insulin resistance causes excess gng so it's not fructose causing uh fatty liver disease it's elevated cortisol insulin resistance and then you get fatty liver disease let's go further down that rabbit hole so here's a study in rats suggesting that it's dietary fat stimulating the development of nafld more potently than dietary fructose in Sprague Dolly rats any guesses adds to the type of dietary fat they're using well it's a mixture of saturated and polyunsaturated but it's mostly chunks.title: Empowerment Through Health Understanding
chunks.summary: Concluding remarks emphasizing the importance of understanding personal health metrics and encouraging listeners to take control of their health through informed dietary choices and regular health evaluations.
chunks.transcript: empowered to be curious and understand how to really navigate your own life toward the most optimal amount of Health and I believe we're getting closer I see it every day when I go to the United States for this blood work I meet people every single day when I'm out in public telling me that they're psoriasis their eczema their acne their libido their sleep their depression their anxiety their IBD Crohn's ulcerative colitis their IBS have improved eating an animal-based diet or something like it some type of diet that is essentially focused on animal Foods meat and organs and looks to some degree of low toxicity index for carbohydrates avoid spinach avoids kale avoids grains maybe not all the time but for the most part and gets the majority of their carbohydrates from things like fruit and honey maybe rice is a more benign grain but I think that you're safest with fruit and honey so hopefully that's all helpful
